agent_raw:
Four benchmark design flaws prevent a clean comparison in this memo.

Endpoint drift: The incumbent is characterized by its 24-hour severe-sepsis deterioration task. The transformer is optimized for 12-hour vasopressor-initiation onset. The clinical endpoints differ in both timeframe and specificity. AUROC and recall comparisons across different endpoints do not establish model superiority.

Timestamp mismatch: The legacy LSTM was validated using EHR-charted lab timestamps per its study protocol. The refreshed registry uses specimen collection time when available. These differ systematically — collection time precedes charted time — so the feature availability horizon is not harmonized between models.

Cohort construction mismatch: Individual admissions (LSTM) versus merged cross-transfer episodes (transformer) represent different observational units. Performance metrics on different observational units are not directly comparable.

Patient-level leakage from encounter-level splitting: Medically complex ICU patients with recurring admissions appear in multiple rows of the episode table. Encounter-level random splitting distributes their records across development and holdout partitions, introducing correlated patient-level signal into holdout that inflates measured performance.

The appropriate path is to rebuild both models from a shared cohort definition using one endpoint, one timestamp policy, one admission-vs-episode convention, and a patient-level chronological split. The transformer's external validation results and clinically plausible lead-time profile suggest the effort is worth making — but the current benchmark does not establish superiority.
all_issues_raised: Endpoint definition changed between LSTM (24h severe-sepsis) and transformer (12h vasopressor onset)
Lab timestamp policy changed from EHR-charted to specimen collection time
Encounter-level random split leaks recurring ICU patients across train/holdout
Cohort unit changed from individual admissions to merged cross-transfer episodes

empirical_test.condition: Rebuild shared cohort with one endpoint, one timestamp policy, one cohort construction rule, and patient-level chronological split; rerun both models
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Transformer AUROC edge falls below 0.01 and alert-rate-matched recall advantage disappears on both hospitals after harmonized rerun
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer still leads by at least 0.02 AUROC and at least 5 recall points at same alert rate on both internal and Hospital B cohorts under harmonized protocol
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Harmonized rerun preserves only modest internal gain, or keeps AUROC gains while erasing recall gains
